content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The easy part got automated. The judgment didn’t.

4 min read
1 hour ago

--

React Compiler is stable in React 20 and Next.js 16. Manual memoization is mostly gone. The part of the job that’s left was always the harder one.
A few weeks after writing about premature memoization, the ground underneath that argument shifted in a way worth naming honestly: most of the manual memoization being criticized doesn’t need to be written by hand anymore. React Compiler, stable since late 2025 and now shipping by default in React 20 and Next.js 16, reads a component at build time and inserts the equivalent of useMemo, useCallback, and React.memo on its own — no annotations, no dependency arrays, no code changes required for components that already follow the Rules of React.
That’s not a small claim, and it isn’t theoretical. Meta has reported double-digit gains on initial load and multiple-times-faster interactions on some of its highest-traffic surfaces after rolling the compiler out internally. Teams running it on well-structured Next.js 16 apps are seeing meaningful cuts in unnecessary re-renders, with the largest gains showing up exactly where you’d expect: codebases that never got around to manual optimization in the first place.
So the easy version of the previous argument — stop reaching for memoization out of reflex — has been overtaken by something better than an argument: a tool that simply does the reaching for you, correctly, every time, for free.

What’s interesting is what didn’t get automated, because it’s the part that was actually the point all along.
The Rules of React used to be a list of habits that made your code easier to reason about. They’re now, functionally, a contract the compiler checks before it will optimize anything. A component that breaks one of those rules in a way the compiler can’t prove safe doesn’t crash and doesn’t warn loudly — it just gets quietly skipped, left exactly as unoptimized as it would have been with no compiler at all. The code still runs. It just stops getting the benefit, silently, and nothing about the running app tells you that happened.
That’s a new shape of invisible cost, and a more dangerous one than the old kind. Forgetting a useCallback produced a problem you could eventually profile your way to. A component the compiler silently bails out on produces nothing — no error, no warning, just a component quietly performing the way it always would have. Right now, the only visibility into this is the lint output, and it tells you what got skipped, not what got optimized. There's still no tool that shows you, component by component, what the compiler actually did — which means trusting the compiler is currently its own kind of judgment call.

A few things genuinely still need a person deciding, precisely because they’re easy to assume the compiler quietly handles too.
Code that intentionally needs to opt out — a third-party integration bending the rules in a way you can’t fix today, or legacy code you’re not ready to verify — has to say so explicitly, rather than hoping the compiler guesses correctly. Data fetching that used to live inside a useEffect, one of the most common sources of the exact bugs covered in the last two pieces here, has a real, purpose-built replacement now in the use() hook, which reads promises and context directly at render time and integrates with Suspense — but moving to it is still a decision someone has to make, component by component, not something the compiler retrofits for you. And underneath all of it, the compiler optimizes re-renders. It has no opinion on whether your component tree is shaped sensibly, whether a list should be virtualized, or whether the actual bottleneck is a network waterfall three layers away from any component at all.

None of this makes the earlier caution about memoization wrong. If anything, it sharpens it. The argument was never really “don’t use useMemo." It was "don't spend your judgment on something a tool could eventually do for you." That tool arrived faster than most predictions had it arriving. The judgment it freed up didn't disappear — it just moved, from a dependency array nobody wanted to maintain, to a quieter and more consequential question: do you actually know what your compiler is doing to your code, or are you just trusting that "stable" means "handled."

If you’ve turned the compiler on and gone looking for what it actually changed, I’d like to hear what you found — or what you couldn’t find a way to check.
